questions.question_text: Under K.S.A. 58-3062, what is the consequence for a licensee who commits substantial misrepresentation in a real estate transaction?
questions.correct_answer: Grounds for license discipline by KREC
distractors: A mandatory fine of $1,000
Automatic license revocation
A warning letter with no further action
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-3062 provides that substantial misrepresentation or fraud in a real estate transaction constitutes grounds for discipline by KREC. Discipline can include suspension, revocation, or other penalties, but the statute does not mandate automatic revocation or a specific fine amount.

questions.question_text: A Kansas listing agent knows the basement floods every spring but tells prospective buyers the basement is 'completely dry year-round.' What violation has occurred?
distractors: A minor puffing exaggeration
A permissible opinion about condition
No violation if the seller approved it
questions.explanation: Recurring basement flooding is a material fact affecting property value and desirability. Stating the basement is 'completely dry year-round' when the agent knows otherwise is a substantial misrepresentation, not mere puffing or opinion. Seller approval does not excuse the agent's duty to disclose material facts.

questions.question_text: A Kansas seller tells her agent to reject any offer below $350,000 without presenting it. The agent receives a $340,000 offer. What must the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Present the offer unless directed otherwise in writing
distractors: Reject it per the seller's verbal instructions
Counter at $350,000 without presenting
Hold the offer until a higher one arrives
questions.explanation: Kansas requires licensees to present all offers to the client. The only exception is when the client has directed otherwise in writing. Verbal instructions to reject offers below a certain price do not satisfy the written direction requirement, so the agent must present the $340,000 offer.

questions.question_text: A Kansas agent knows a neighboring property is a former landfill that may affect groundwater. The seller says nothing about it. Must the agent disclose this to prospective buyers?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, it is a material fact affecting value
distractors: No, only the seller has a disclosure duty
No, neighboring properties are irrelevant
Only if the buyer asks about environmental issues
questions.explanation: Kansas licensees must disclose material facts that affect the value or desirability of a property. A neighboring former landfill that may contaminate groundwater is a material fact. The agent's independent duty to disclose exists regardless of whether the seller mentions it or the buyer asks.

questions.question_text: A Kansas agent tells a buyer that a property 'probably' has no termite damage despite noticing suspicious wood damage in the crawl space. The buyer relies on this statement and purchases the home. What type of violation is this?
distractors: No violation because the agent said 'probably'
A violation only if the agent is a termite expert
Permissible opinion about property condition
questions.explanation: Downplaying observed wood damage by suggesting 'probably' no termite issue is a misrepresentation of a material fact. The qualifier 'probably' does not shield the agent from liability when the agent observed evidence suggesting otherwise. The agent should have disclosed the suspicious damage and recommended a professional inspection.

questions.question_text: A Kansas licensee wants to purchase a property listed by her own brokerage. What must she disclose?
questions.correct_answer: Her personal interest as a licensee
distractors: Nothing, if she pays full listing price
Only her license number on the contract
Her interest only if she negotiates a discount
questions.explanation: Kansas prohibits licensees from failing to disclose a personal interest in a transaction. When a licensee wants to purchase a property, the licensee's status and personal interest must be disclosed to all parties. Paying full price or any other consideration does not eliminate the disclosure requirement.

questions.question_text: A Kansas agent represents a buyer purchasing a duplex. The agent's brother owns the duplex, but the agent never discloses this family relationship in the contract. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Failure to disclose a family member's interest
distractors: No violation because the agent is not the owner
A violation only if the price is above market value
A violation only if the brother holds a license
questions.explanation: K.A.R. 86-3-19 requires a licensee to disclose in the contract any interest the licensee or an immediate family member — spouse, parent, child, or sibling — has in the real estate being sold or purchased. The agent's brother is an immediate family member, so his ownership must be disclosed regardless of who holds title or whether the price is fair.

questions.question_text: A Kansas broker proposes to act as a dual agent, personally representing both the buyer and the seller in the same transaction. Can she do this?
questions.correct_answer: No, dual agency is prohibited in Kansas
distractors: Yes, with written consent of both parties
Yes, if she rebates part of her commission
Yes, if both parties are experienced investors
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-30,103(a) flatly prohibits a licensee from acting as a dual agent — no disclosure or consent can authorize it. In-house transactions must instead use designated agency or transaction brokerage; a firm acting as transaction broker for both sides needs informed consent evidenced by a signed transaction broker addendum (K.S.A. 58-30,109).

questions.question_text: A Kansas licensee forms an LLC to buy a foreclosure and does not reveal in the purchase contract that she is a licensee with an ownership interest in the LLC. Has she violated any Kansas rule?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, her interest must be disclosed in the contract
distractors: No, because the LLC is a separate legal entity from her
No, if her supervising broker knows about the purchase
No, because foreclosure sales are exempt from disclosure
questions.explanation: K.A.R. 86-3-19 and K.S.A. 58-3062(a)(15) require a licensee to make known, in the contract, any interest the licensee has or will have in real estate being purchased. Buying through an LLC does not eliminate the duty, and telling only the supervising broker does not satisfy it.

questions.question_text: A Kansas agent owns a 25% share in a rental management company. A buyer client asks about property management options, and the agent recommends only her own company without disclosing her ownership. What rule applies?
distractors: No rule applies to ancillary services
Disclosure is needed only for 50%+ ownership
The recommendation is permitted as market advice
questions.explanation: An agent who steers a client toward a company the agent partly owns, without revealing the ownership, violates the duty to promote the client's interests with utmost good faith, loyalty and fidelity (K.S.A. 58-30,107). No minimum ownership threshold applies, and any resulting undisclosed compensation would also violate K.S.A. 58-3062(a)(3).

questions.question_text: A Kansas agent contacts a homeowner who has an exclusive listing with another brokerage and urges the owner to terminate that listing. What prohibited act has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Inducing breach of an agency agreement
distractors: Permissible competitive solicitation
A violation only if the listing has expired
Improper advertising to the public
questions.explanation: Kansas prohibits licensees from inducing breach of an existing agency agreement. Urging a homeowner to terminate an exclusive listing with another brokerage constitutes inducement of breach. This is not permissible competitive solicitation, which is limited to situations where no exclusive agreement exists.

questions.question_text: After both parties sign a purchase agreement, a Kansas listing agent provides a copy to the seller but not the buyer. What obligation has the agent failed to meet?
questions.correct_answer: Providing copies of signed documents to all parties
distractors: No obligation because the buyer has their own agent
No obligation until the transaction closes
An obligation only if the buyer requests a copy
questions.explanation: Kansas requires licensees to provide copies of signed documents to all parties who signed them. This duty exists regardless of whether the buyer has separate representation, whether the transaction has closed, or whether the buyer specifically requests a copy.

questions.question_text: Which standard of conduct does Kansas require of all real estate licensees in their dealings?
distractors: Strict liability for all property defects
Fiduciary duty to all transaction parties
Caveat emptor with no disclosure duty
questions.explanation: Kansas law requires all licensees to act honestly and in good faith in their real estate dealings. This is a baseline standard that applies to all parties in a transaction. It does not impose strict liability for defects or fiduciary duties to all parties, but it does exceed a pure caveat emptor standard.

questions.question_text: A Kansas agent learns that a homeowner has an active exclusive listing with another brokerage that still has four months remaining. The agent sends the homeowner a marketing packet and suggests they could get a better price. Has a violation occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, inducing breach of an existing listing
distractors: No, sending information is fair competition
No, the homeowner can cancel at any time
A violation only if the homeowner terminates
questions.explanation: Soliciting a homeowner who has an active exclusive listing with another brokerage and suggesting superior results constitutes inducing breach of an existing agency agreement. This is prohibited regardless of whether the homeowner actually terminates. Competitive solicitation is only permitted when no exclusive agreement is in effect.

questions.question_text: Both parties have signed a counteroffer in a Kansas transaction. Under K.S.A. 58-3062, when must the licensee provide copies of the signed document to the parties?
questions.correct_answer: Within a reasonable time after signing
distractors: Only at the closing of the transaction
Within 30 days after the contract date
Only when a party requests a copy in writing
questions.explanation: K.S.A. 58-3062(a)(11) prohibits failing to provide copies of written agreements to all parties involved within a reasonable time. The duty applies to every party who signed, whether or not anyone requests a copy, and does not wait until closing.
